Maths - Number and operations.skill: Number and operations - extend previous knowledge of sets and subsets using a visual representation to describe relationships between sets of real numbers
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers classifying real numbers using sets and subsets for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.2(A)). Students use Venn diagrams and other visual representations to show relationships among natural numbers, whole numbers, integers, rational numbers, and irrational numbers. Key concepts include: understanding that each number set is a subset of a larger set, identifying which sets a given number belongs to, and recognizing that rational and irrational numbers together form the real numbers. Vocabulary: natural numbers, whole numbers, integers, rational numbers, irrational numbers, real numbers, set, subset, Venn diagram. Common misconceptions: thinking all decimals are irrational, not recognizing repeating decimals as rational, and confusing the hierarchy. Practice should include classification exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Number and operations - approximate the value of an irrational number, including π and square roots of numbers less than 225, and locate that rational number approximation on a number line
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers approximating irrational numbers for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.2(B)). Students approximate values of irrational numbers including pi and square roots less than 225, and locate them on number lines. Key concepts include: understanding that irrational numbers cannot be expressed as fractions, estimating square roots between consecutive integers, refining estimates using decimals, and placing approximations on number lines. Vocabulary: irrational number, pi, square root, approximate, estimate, number line, rational approximation. Common misconceptions: thinking pi equals exactly 3.14 and not understanding that irrational numbers have non-repeating, non-terminating decimals. Practice should include estimation and number line placement appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Number and operations - Estimate positive square roots
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers estimating positive square roots for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.2(B)). Students estimate square roots of non-perfect squares by identifying the two consecutive perfect squares the number falls between. Key concepts include: recognizing perfect squares (1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ..., 225), determining between which two integers a square root falls, refining estimates, and understanding the relationship between squaring and square roots. Vocabulary: square root, perfect square, estimate, consecutive integers, rational approximation. Common misconceptions: confusing squaring with doubling and thinking all square roots are irrational. Practice should include estimation exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Number and operations - Estimate positive and negative square roots
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers estimating both positive and negative square roots for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.2(B)). Students estimate square roots including negative results, understanding that every positive number has two square roots. Key concepts include: positive and negative square roots, the principal (positive) square root, estimating between consecutive integers, and understanding that negative numbers have no real square roots. Vocabulary: square root, positive, negative, principal square root, perfect square, estimate, real number. Common misconceptions: forgetting the negative square root, thinking negative numbers have real square roots, and confusing the negative of a square root with the square root of a negative. Practice should include estimation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Number and operations - Irrational numbers on number lines
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers placing irrational numbers on number lines for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.2(B)). Students approximate irrational numbers and locate them accurately on number lines. Key concepts include: estimating square roots and pi, determining approximate positions between integers, comparing and ordering irrational numbers with rational numbers, and understanding the density of real numbers. Vocabulary: irrational number, number line, approximate, estimate, square root, pi, position, between. Common misconceptions: placing irrational numbers at exact integer positions and not understanding the precision needed. Practice should include number line placement exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Number and operations - convert between standard decimal notation and scientific notation
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers converting between standard and scientific notation for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.2(C)). Students express very large and very small numbers in scientific notation (a x 10^n where 1 <= a < 10). Key concepts include: moving the decimal point and adjusting the exponent, positive exponents for large numbers, negative exponents for small numbers, and understanding why scientific notation is useful. Vocabulary: scientific notation, standard notation, exponent, power of 10, coefficient, decimal point. Common misconceptions: not keeping the coefficient between 1 and 10, confusing direction of decimal movement with exponent sign, and errors with negative exponents. Practice should include both directions of conversion appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Number and operations - Compare numbers written in scientific notation
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers comparing numbers in scientific notation for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.2(C)). Students compare and order numbers expressed in scientific notation. Key concepts include: first comparing exponents (larger exponent means larger number for positive coefficients), then comparing coefficients when exponents are equal, and handling negative exponents. Vocabulary: scientific notation, compare, order, exponent, coefficient, greater than, less than. Common misconceptions: comparing only coefficients without considering exponents and errors with negative exponents. Practice should include ordering sets of numbers in scientific notation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Number and operations - order a set of real numbers arising from mathematical and real-world contexts
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers ordering real numbers for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.2(D)). Students compare and order sets of real numbers that include rational numbers (fractions, decimals, integers) and irrational numbers (square roots, pi). Key concepts include: converting all numbers to decimal approximations for comparison, placing numbers on a number line, and using inequality symbols. Vocabulary: real numbers, rational, irrational, compare, order, number line, inequality, approximate. Common misconceptions: not approximating irrational numbers before comparing and difficulty comparing fractions with square roots. Practice should include mixed sets of number types appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers ordering real numbers for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.2(D)). Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic is a checkpoint assessment on scientific notation for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.2(C)). Students demonstrate mastery of converting between standard and scientific notation, comparing numbers in scientific notation, and applying scientific notation in context. Key concepts include: proper coefficient range (1 <= a < 10), exponent rules, and ordering numbers in scientific notation. Vocabulary: scientific notation, standard notation, exponent, coefficient, power of 10, compare. Assessment should cover conversions, comparisons, and applications appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - generalize that the ratio of corresponding sides of similar shapes are proportional, including a shape and its dilation
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers Proportionality - generalize that the ratio of corresponding sides of similar shapes are proportional, including a shape and its dilation for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(A)). This aligns with TEKS standard 8.3(A). Students at this level should develop proficiency in this skill, building on prior knowledge and connecting to grade-level expectations. Key concepts include: understanding the underlying mathematical principles, applying appropriate procedures and strategies, solving both computational and contextual problems, and justifying solutions using mathematical reasoning. Students should represent the concept using multiple representations (numerical, graphical, verbal, algebraic) and make connections to real-world applications. Vocabulary and notation specific to this topic should be used precisely. Common misconceptions should be addressed through careful instruction and varied practice. Problems and explanations should be appropriate for 13-14 year olds at the Grade 8 level, progressing from concrete to abstract understanding.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Similar and congruent figures
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers similar and congruent figures for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(A)). Students distinguish between similar figures (same shape, proportional sides) and congruent figures (same shape and size). Key concepts include: corresponding sides and angles, proportional relationships in similar figures, congruence as a special case of similarity with scale factor 1, and transformations that produce each type. Vocabulary: similar, congruent, corresponding sides, corresponding angles, proportional, scale factor, transformation. Common misconceptions: confusing similar with congruent and thinking similarity requires equal sides. Practice should include identification and comparison appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Identify similar triangles
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers identifying similar triangles for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(A)). Students determine whether two triangles are similar by checking proportional sides or equal angles. Key concepts include: AA (angle-angle) similarity criterion, SSS similarity (all corresponding sides proportional), SAS similarity, and computing scale factors. Vocabulary: similar triangles, AA criterion, corresponding sides, corresponding angles, scale factor, proportional. Common misconceptions: requiring all three angles to be checked when two suffice and confusing similarity with congruence. Practice should include identification from given measurements appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Side lengths and angle measures of similar figures
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers finding side lengths and angle measures of similar figures for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(A)). Students use proportional relationships to find unknown measurements in similar figures. Key concepts include: setting up proportions with corresponding sides, understanding that corresponding angles are equal in similar figures, and computing scale factors. Vocabulary: similar figures, corresponding, proportional, scale factor, angle, side length, ratio. Common misconceptions: setting up proportions with non-corresponding sides, not recognizing which sides correspond, and thinking angles change. Practice should include finding missing measurements appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Similar solids
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers similar solids for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(A)). Students extend similarity concepts to three-dimensional figures. Key concepts include: corresponding dimensions are proportional, scale factor applies to all linear dimensions, surface area scales by k^2, volume scales by k^3, and setting up proportions. Vocabulary: similar solids, scale factor, corresponding, proportional, surface area ratio, volume ratio, cube, square. Common misconceptions: applying the linear scale factor directly to surface area or volume, and not understanding the squared and cubed relationships. Practice should include 3D similarity problems appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - compare and contrast the attributes of a shape and its dilation(s) on a coordinate plane
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers comparing attributes of a shape and its dilation for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(B)). Students analyze how dilation changes a figure on a coordinate plane. Key concepts include: dilated figures are similar to the original, side lengths change by the scale factor, angles remain the same, area changes by the square of the scale factor, and the center of dilation affects position. Vocabulary: dilation, scale factor, similar, coordinate plane, center of dilation, attribute, preserve, proportional. Common misconceptions: thinking angles change during dilation and confusing enlargement with reduction. Practice should include coordinate plane dilations appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Dilations: find the scale factor
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers finding the scale factor for dilations for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(B)). Students determine the scale factor by comparing corresponding measurements of a figure and its dilation. Key concepts include: scale factor = image length / original length, scale factor > 1 means enlargement, 0 < scale factor < 1 means reduction, and using coordinates to find scale factors. Vocabulary: dilation, scale factor, image, pre-image, enlargement, reduction, corresponding, ratio. Common misconceptions: dividing in the wrong order and confusing enlargement with reduction. Practice should include coordinate and non-coordinate problems appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - use an algebraic representation to explain the effect of a given positive rational scale factor applied to two-dimensional figures on a coordinate plane with the origin as the center of dilation
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers algebraic representations of dilations for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(C)). Students use coordinate rules (x, y) -> (kx, ky) to describe dilations with the origin as center. Key concepts include: applying the scale factor k to both coordinates, understanding k > 1 enlarges and 0 < k < 1 reduces, the effect on the figure's position relative to the origin, and connecting the algebraic rule to the geometric transformation. Vocabulary: dilation, scale factor, algebraic representation, coordinate rule, origin, center of dilation, image. Common misconceptions: not applying k to both coordinates and not understanding the origin as center. Practice should include applying algebraic rules appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Dilations: graph the image
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers graphing dilated images for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(C)). Students graph the image of a figure after dilation on a coordinate plane. Key concepts include: multiplying each coordinate by the scale factor, plotting the new vertices, connecting to form the image, and verifying similarity. Vocabulary: dilation, scale factor, image, pre-image, coordinate plane, vertex, graph. Common misconceptions: not applying the scale factor to both x and y, and errors with fractional scale factors. Practice should include graphing on coordinate planes appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Dilations: find the coordinates
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers finding coordinates after dilation for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(C)). Students calculate the coordinates of a figure's image after dilation using (x, y) -> (kx, ky). Key concepts include: multiplying both coordinates by the scale factor, working with integer and fractional scale factors, and identifying the center of dilation. Vocabulary: dilation, scale factor, coordinates, image, pre-image, origin, center of dilation. Common misconceptions: applying the scale factor to only one coordinate and arithmetic errors with fractions. Practice should include various scale factors appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Checkpoint: Dilations and similarity
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic is a checkpoint assessment on dilations and similarity for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.3(A)). Students demonstrate mastery of identifying similar figures, finding scale factors, performing dilations on coordinate planes, and understanding the relationship between dilations and similarity. Key concepts: proportional sides, equal angles, scale factor calculations, coordinate transformations, and the algebraic rule (x,y) -> (kx, ky). Vocabulary: dilation, similarity, scale factor, corresponding, proportional. Assessment should cover identification, computation, and graphing appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - use similar right triangles to develop an understanding that slope, m, given as the rate comparing the change in y-values to the change in x-values, (y2 - y1)/ (x2 - x1), is the same for any two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) on the same line
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers using similar right triangles to understand slope for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(A)). Students discover that the slope between any two points on a line is the same by forming right triangles and showing they are similar. Key concepts include: slope as rise/run or (y2-y1)/(x2-x1), similar triangles formed by any two points on the same line have equal ratios of vertical to horizontal change, and slope is constant for a line. Vocabulary: slope, similar triangles, rise, run, ratio, constant, linear, change in y, change in x. Common misconceptions: thinking slope can change along a line and confusing the order of subtraction. Practice should include geometric slope exploration appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the line that models the relationship
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers graphing proportional relationships and interpreting slope as unit rate for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(B)). Students graph proportional relationships and understand that the slope of the line equals the unit rate (constant of proportionality). Key concepts include: proportional graphs pass through the origin, slope = k in y = kx, reading the unit rate from the graph at x = 1, and comparing slopes of different proportional relationships. Vocabulary: proportional relationship, slope, unit rate, constant of proportionality, origin, graph, y = kx. Common misconceptions: confusing slope with y-intercept and not recognizing that proportional graphs must pass through (0,0). Practice should include graphing and interpretation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Find the constant of proportionality from a graph
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers finding the constant of proportionality from a graph for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(B)). Students determine k in y = kx by reading a point from the graph and computing y/x. Key concepts include: choosing clear points, computing k = y/x, understanding k as slope through the origin, and using the unit rate point (1, k). Vocabulary: constant of proportionality, graph, slope, origin, unit rate, y = kx. Common misconceptions: reading coordinates incorrectly, using x/y, and selecting points not on the line. Practice should include graphs with various scales appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Graph proportional relationships and find the slope
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers graphing proportional relationships and finding slope for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(B)). Students plot proportional data and determine the slope of the resulting line. Key concepts include: plotting ordered pairs, drawing the line through origin, calculating slope as rise/run or y/x, and interpreting slope as the unit rate. Vocabulary: proportional, graph, slope, rise, run, unit rate, origin, coordinate plane. Common misconceptions: not passing the line through the origin and errors in slope calculation. Practice should include graphing and slope computation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - use data from a table or graph to determine the rate of change or slope and y-intercept in mathematical and real-world problems
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers determining slope and y-intercept from tables and graphs for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(C)). Students extract rate of change (slope) and initial value (y-intercept) from data representations. Key concepts include: slope from a table as change in y / change in x between rows, slope from a graph as rise/run, y-intercept as the value when x = 0, and interpreting both in context. Vocabulary: slope, y-intercept, rate of change, initial value, table, graph, rise, run, linear. Common misconceptions: using only one data point for slope, confusing x-intercept with y-intercept, and not interpreting in context. Practice should include tables and graphs from real-world problems appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Find the constant of proportionality from a table
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers finding the constant of proportionality from a table for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(C)). Students calculate k in y = kx by dividing y-values by corresponding x-values. Key concepts include: computing y/x for each row, verifying consistency, understanding k as the unit rate, and writing the equation. Vocabulary: constant of proportionality, table, ratio, y = kx, unit rate. Common misconceptions: computing x/y instead of y/x, not checking all rows, and confusing additive with multiplicative patterns. Practice should include real-world tables appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Find the constant of proportionality from a graph
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers finding the constant of proportionality from a graph for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(C)). Students determine k in y = kx by reading a point from the graph and computing y/x. Key concepts include: choosing clear points, computing k = y/x, understanding k as slope through the origin, and using the unit rate point (1, k). Vocabulary: constant of proportionality, graph, slope, origin, unit rate, y = kx. Common misconceptions: reading coordinates incorrectly, using x/y, and selecting points not on the line. Practice should include graphs with various scales appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Find the slope from a graph
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers finding slope from a graph for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(C)). Students calculate the slope of a line by identifying two points and computing rise over run. Key concepts include: choosing two clear points, calculating vertical change (rise) and horizontal change (run), dividing rise by run, and determining if slope is positive, negative, zero, or undefined. Vocabulary: slope, rise, run, graph, positive slope, negative slope, zero slope, undefined slope. Common misconceptions: computing run/rise instead of rise/run, not accounting for negative slopes, and reading coordinates incorrectly. Practice should include lines with various slopes appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Find the slope from a table
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers finding slope from a table for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(C)). Students calculate slope by finding the ratio of change in y-values to change in x-values between table entries. Key concepts include: selecting any two rows, computing (y2-y1)/(x2-x1), verifying with multiple pairs for linear data, and interpreting the slope in context. Vocabulary: slope, table, rate of change, change in y, change in x, ratio, linear. Common misconceptions: subtracting in inconsistent order, only checking one pair, and confusing slope with y-values. Practice should include tables from real-world contexts appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Rate of change of a linear function: tables
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers rate of change of linear functions from tables for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(C)). Students determine whether a table represents a linear function by checking for a constant rate of change. Key concepts include: computing change in y / change in x for consecutive entries, verifying constancy, interpreting the rate in context, and connecting to the slope of the line. Vocabulary: rate of change, linear function, constant, table, slope, proportional. Common misconceptions: assuming all tables are linear and only checking one pair of rows. Practice should include identifying linear and non-linear tables appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Rate of change of a linear function: graphs
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers rate of change of linear functions from graphs for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(C)). Students identify the rate of change from graphs and determine if it is constant (linear). Key concepts include: constant rate of change produces a straight line, computing slope between points, comparing rates at different parts of the graph, and connecting to the equation. Vocabulary: rate of change, linear function, constant, graph, slope, straight line. Common misconceptions: confusing steepness with constant rate and assuming curved graphs have constant rates. Practice should include varied graph analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Interpret the slope and y-intercept of a linear function
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers interpreting slope and y-intercept in context for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(C)). Students explain the real-world meaning of slope (rate of change) and y-intercept (initial value) of linear functions. Key concepts include: slope as how fast y changes per unit of x, y-intercept as the starting value when x = 0, and connecting both to the equation y = mx + b. Vocabulary: slope, y-intercept, rate of change, initial value, linear function, interpret, context. Common misconceptions: reversing the meanings of slope and y-intercept and not connecting to real-world context. Practice should include contextual interpretation problems appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Maths - Proportionality.skill: Proportionality - Write a linear function from a table
Maths - Proportionality.prompt: This topic covers writing a linear function from a table for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.4(C)). Students determine the equation of a linear function from table data. Key concepts include: finding slope from the table, identifying the y-intercept (value when x=0), and writing the equation in y = mx + b form. Vocabulary: linear function, slope, y-intercept, table, equation, y = mx + b, rate of change. Common misconceptions: errors in computing slope, not finding the y-intercept correctly when x=0 is not in the table, and confusing slope with y-intercept. Practice should include tables from real-world contexts appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Laboratory safety: Demonstrate safe practices during laboratory and field investigations as outlined in TEA-approved safety standards

Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers laboratory safety practices for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(C)). Students demonstrate safe practices during laboratory and field investigations following TEA-approved safety standards. Key concepts include: wearing PPE (goggles, aprons, gloves), proper chemical and specimen handling, knowing safety equipment locations (fire extinguisher, eyewash station, fire blanket), following instructions, and reporting accidents. Students practice appropriate use and conservation of materials including proper disposal, reuse, and recycling. Key concepts include: minimizing waste, properly disposing chemicals and biological materials, reusing when safe, recycling, and environmental impact awareness. Vocabulary: conservation, disposal, reuse, recycle, waste, resource, environmental impact, sustainable, hazardous waste. Common misconceptions: thinking all lab waste goes in regular trash. Practice should include lab cleanup scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Scientific investigations: Plan and implement comparative and descriptive investigations by making observations, asking well-defined questions, and using appropriate equipment and technology
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific and engineering practices to plan and conduct investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers planning comparative and descriptive investigations for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students design investigations that compare groups or describe phenomena. Key concepts include: comparative investigations (comparing groups) vs. descriptive (observing without manipulation), systematic observations, clear research questions, and appropriate equipment. Vocabulary: comparative investigation, descriptive investigation, observation, research question, equipment, data, systematic, variable. Common misconceptions: confusing descriptive with experimental and not controlling variables. Practice should include designing both types appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Scientific investigations: The process of scientific inquiry
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Ask questions and define problems based on observations or information
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers the process of scientific inquiry for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.1(A)). Students understand the steps and nature of scientific investigation: observing, asking testable questions, forming hypotheses, designing experiments, collecting/analyzing data, drawing conclusions, and communicating results. Vocabulary: observation, hypothesis, experiment, data, conclusion, variable, control, testable question, evidence. Common misconceptions: thinking there is one rigid scientific method and that hypotheses must be correct. Practice should include identifying inquiry steps appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Scientific investigations: Identify laboratory tools
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use appropriate laboratory tools
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying laboratory tools for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(D)). Students recognize common lab instruments and their uses: graduated cylinder (liquid volume), beaker (holding liquids), triple beam balance (mass), thermometer (temperature), microscope (magnification), spring scale (force), hot plate, test tubes, Erlenmeyer flask, forceps. Vocabulary: graduated cylinder, beaker, triple beam balance, thermometer, microscope, spring scale, Erlenmeyer flask, pipette, forceps. Common misconceptions: confusing beakers with graduated cylinders for measurement and misreading meniscus. Practice should include identification exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Design and implement experimental investigations by making observations, asking well-defined questions, formulating testable hypotheses, and using appropriate equipment and technology
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific and engineering practices to plan and conduct investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers designing and implementing experimental investigations for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students create experiments with testable hypotheses, controlled variables, and appropriate methodology. Key concepts include: formulating testable hypotheses, identifying independent/dependent/controlled variables, designing procedures, selecting equipment, collecting data, and drawing evidence-based conclusions. Vocabulary: experiment, hypothesis, independent variable, dependent variable, controlled variable, procedure, data, conclusion. Common misconceptions: not controlling variables, confusing correlation with causation, and writing untestable hypotheses. Practice should include experimental design scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Identify control and experimental groups
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific and engineering practices to plan and conduct investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying control and experimental groups for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students distinguish between the group that receives the treatment (experimental) and the group that serves as the baseline (control). Key concepts include: the control group provides a comparison standard, the experimental group receives the independent variable treatment, keeping all other conditions the same, and understanding why controls are necessary. Vocabulary: control group, experimental group, independent variable, treatment, baseline, comparison, constant. Common misconceptions: thinking the control group is unnecessary and confusing which group receives the treatment. Practice should include identifying groups in scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Identify independent and dependent variables
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific and engineering practices to plan investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying independent and dependent variables in scientific investigations for Grade 8 students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students distinguish between the variable changed by the experimenter (independent) and the variable measured as a response (dependent). Key concepts include: the independent variable is what is changed on purpose, the dependent variable is what is measured/observed, controlled variables are kept constant, and the dependent variable depends on the independent variable. Vocabulary: independent variable, dependent variable, controlled variable, constant, experiment, respond, manipulate. Common misconceptions: reversing independent and dependent and forgetting controlled variables. Practice should include identifying variables in experiments appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Identify the experimental question
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Ask questions and define problems based on observations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying the experimental question for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(A)). Students formulate clear, testable questions that can be investigated through experiments. Key concepts include: a good experimental question identifies what is being tested, specifies the variables, can be answered through data collection, and leads to a clear hypothesis. Vocabulary: experimental question, testable, hypothesis, variable, investigation, predict, observe. Common misconceptions: writing questions that are too broad or untestable and confusing questions with hypotheses. Practice should include evaluating and writing questions appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Identify questions that can be investigated with a set of materials
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Ask questions and define problems based on observations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying questions that can be investigated with given materials for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(A)). Students evaluate which questions are testable given a specific set of materials and equipment. Key concepts include: matching questions to available resources, understanding limitations of materials, designing feasible investigations, and distinguishing testable from untestable questions. Vocabulary: testable, investigation, materials, equipment, feasible, limitation, observation, measurement. Common misconceptions: thinking any question can be tested with any materials and not considering practical constraints. Practice should include evaluating questions against material lists appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Understand an experimental protocol about plant growth
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers understanding experimental protocols about plant growth for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students analyze and interpret a detailed experimental procedure studying factors affecting plant growth. Key concepts include: identifying variables (light, water, soil, fertilizer), understanding the control setup, interpreting data tables and graphs of growth measurements, and drawing conclusions about what factors affect plant growth. Vocabulary: protocol, plant growth, variable, control, measurement, data, conclusion, photosynthesis, germination. Common misconceptions: thinking plants only need sunlight (forgetting water, nutrients) and not controlling variables properly. Practice should include protocol analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Understand an experimental protocol about diffusion
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers understanding experimental protocols about diffusion for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students analyze experiments studying the movement of particles from high to low concentration. Key concepts include: diffusion as passive movement of molecules, concentration gradients, factors affecting diffusion rate (temperature, concentration difference, particle size), experimental setup with indicators, and data interpretation. Vocabulary: diffusion, concentration, gradient, particles, molecules, membrane, indicator, rate, passive transport. Common misconceptions: thinking diffusion requires energy and that it stops when equilibrium is reached (it continues but net movement is zero). Practice should include protocol analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Understand an experimental protocol about evaporation
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers understanding experimental protocols about evaporation for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(B)). Students analyze experiments investigating factors affecting evaporation rate. Key concepts include: evaporation as the process of liquid becoming gas at the surface, factors affecting rate (temperature, surface area, humidity, wind), designing controlled experiments, measuring volume change over time, and interpreting data. Vocabulary: evaporation, surface area, temperature, humidity, rate, volume, control variable, water cycle. Common misconceptions: confusing evaporation with boiling and thinking evaporation only occurs at high temperatures. Practice should include protocol analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Data collection: Collect and record data using the International System of Units (SI) and qualitative means such as labeled drawings, writing, and graphic organizers
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Collect quantitative and qualitative data using SI units
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers collecting and recording data using SI units for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(E)). Students use the International System of Units (metric system) and qualitative methods to collect and organize data. Key concepts include: using SI units (meters, kilograms, liters, degrees Celsius), recording data in organized tables, using labeled drawings and graphic organizers, distinguishing quantitative from qualitative data, and appropriate significant figures. Vocabulary: SI units, metric system, meter, kilogram, liter, Celsius, qualitative, quantitative, data table. Common misconceptions: confusing SI with customary units and not recording units with measurements. Practice should include data collection activities appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Data collection: Choose metric units of distance, mass, and volume

Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers choosing appropriate metric units for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(E)). Students select the correct metric unit for measuring distance, mass, and volume in various situations. Key units include: distance (millimeters, centimeters, meters, kilometers), mass (milligrams, grams, kilograms), volume (milliliters, liters), and understanding the relative sizes of each. Vocabulary: metric, meter, centimeter, millimeter, kilometer, gram, kilogram, milligram, liter, milliliter, unit. Common misconceptions: using inappropriate units (e.g., kilometers for pencil length) and confusing mass with weight. Practice should include matching units to measurements appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Data analysis: Construct tables and graphs, using repeated trials and means, to organize data and identify patterns

Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers constructing tables and graphs for data analysis for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(F)). Students organize data into tables and graphs to identify patterns. Key concepts include: creating data tables with proper labels and units, choosing appropriate graph types (bar, line, circle), using repeated trials and calculating means, labeling axes and titles, and identifying trends and patterns in graphed data. Vocabulary: data table, graph, bar graph, line graph, axis, label, title, mean, trend, pattern, trial. Common misconceptions: not labeling axes, choosing inappropriate graph types, and not using consistent scales. Practice should include data organization exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Data analysis: Use data from tests to compare engineering-design solutions

Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers using data to compare engineering design solutions for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.2(D)). Vocabulary: engineering design, criteria, constraints, data, compare, evaluate, trade-off, solution, performance, evidence. Common misconceptions: choosing designs based on preference rather than data and not considering all criteria. Practice should include design comparison scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers analyzing data to formulate explanations and predict trends for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.2(B)). Common misconceptions: confusing correlation with causation, making claims not supported by data, and over-generalizing from limited data. Practice should include data interpretation scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Models: Label Earth features at tectonic plate boundaries
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers labeling Earth features at tectonic plate boundaries for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(G)). Students identify and label features formed at different plate boundary types. Key features include: mountains (convergent), volcanoes (convergent/hot spots), ocean trenches (subduction), mid-ocean ridges (divergent), rift valleys (divergent), and fault lines (transform). Key concepts include: connecting each feature to the type of plate interaction that creates it. Vocabulary: convergent boundary, divergent boundary, transform boundary, trench, mid-ocean ridge, rift valley, subduction zone, fault, mountain chain. Common misconceptions: confusing feature types and not connecting features to boundary types. Practice should include diagram labeling appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Models: Analyze models of the Earth-Sun-Moon system
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Identify advantages and limitations of models
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers analyzing models of the Earth-Sun-Moon system for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.2(A)). Students evaluate models that represent the relationships and movements within this system. Key concepts include: Earth's rotation (day/night), Earth's revolution around the Sun (seasons), Moon's revolution around Earth (lunar phases), eclipses, and the advantages and limitations of various models. Vocabulary: rotation, revolution, orbit, axis, tilt, eclipse, lunar phases, model, scale, limitation. Common misconceptions: thinking seasons are caused by distance from the Sun, confusing rotation with revolution, and not recognizing model limitations. Practice should include model evaluation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Models: Identify phases of the Moon
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying Moon phases for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(G)). Students recognize and name the eight phases of the Moon from visual representations. Key phases: new moon (not visible), waxing crescent, first quarter (right half illuminated), waxing gibbous, full moon, waning gibbous, third quarter (left half illuminated), waning crescent. Key concepts include: the illuminated portion changes predictably, waxing means growing, waning means shrinking. Vocabulary: new moon, waxing crescent, first quarter, waxing gibbous, full moon, waning gibbous, third quarter, waning crescent. Common misconceptions: confusing first and third quarter, reversing waxing and waning, and thinking the Moon produces its own light. Practice should include phase identification appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers what causes seasons on Earth for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(G)). Students understand that Earth's axial tilt, not distance from the Sun, causes seasons. Key concepts include: the 23.5-degree tilt causes different amounts of direct sunlight at different times of year, summer occurs when a hemisphere tilts toward the Sun, direct sunlight is more intense than angled sunlight, and the equator has relatively constant seasons. Vocabulary: seasons, axial tilt, direct sunlight, indirect sunlight, hemisphere, solstice, equinox, summer, winter. Common misconceptions: thinking seasons are caused by changing distance from the Sun, that the entire Earth has the same season, and confusing weather with seasons. Practice should include seasonal analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Advantages and limitations of models: Analyze models of the Earth-Sun-Moon system
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Identify advantages and limitations of models
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers analyzing models of the Earth-Sun-Moon system for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.2(A)). Students evaluate models that represent the relationships and movements within this system. Key concepts include: Earth's rotation (day/night), Earth's revolution around the Sun (seasons), Moon's revolution around Earth (lunar phases), eclipses, and the advantages and limitations of various models. Vocabulary: rotation, revolution, orbit, axis, tilt, eclipse, lunar phases, model, scale, limitation. Common misconceptions: thinking seasons are caused by distance from the Sun, confusing rotation with revolution, and not recognizing model limitations. Practice should include model evaluation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Impact of research: Explore the engineering-design process: going to the Moon!
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Relate impact of research on scientific thought and society
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers the engineering design process through the context of lunar exploration for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.4(A)). Students explore how engineering design was applied to space missions. Key concepts include: the engineering design process (identify problem, research, design, build, test, redesign), challenges of space travel (no atmosphere, extreme temperatures, radiation, microgravity), historical Moon missions, and how scientific research impacts society. Vocabulary: engineering design, prototype, criteria, constraint, iteration, spacecraft, lunar, mission, technology. Common misconceptions: thinking engineering design is linear and that first designs are always final. Practice should include design challenge activities appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Laboratory tools: Identify laboratory tools
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use appropriate laboratory tools
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying laboratory tools for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(D)). Students recognize common lab instruments and their uses: graduated cylinder (liquid volume), beaker (holding liquids), triple beam balance (mass), thermometer (temperature), microscope (magnification), spring scale (force), hot plate, test tubes, Erlenmeyer flask, forceps. Vocabulary: graduated cylinder, beaker, triple beam balance, thermometer, microscope, spring scale, Erlenmeyer flask, pipette, forceps. Common misconceptions: confusing beakers with graduated cylinders for measurement and misreading meniscus. Practice should include identification exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Safety equipment: Laboratory safety equipment
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use appropriate safety equipment and practices during investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers laboratory safety equipment for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.1(C)). Students identify the purpose and proper use of common safety equipment in the laboratory. Key equipment includes: safety goggles (eye protection), lab apron (body protection), fire extinguisher, fire blanket, eyewash station, safety shower, fume hood, first aid kit, and broken glass container. Key concepts include: knowing when each piece of equipment is needed, proper usage procedures, and equipment locations. Vocabulary: safety goggles, apron, fire extinguisher, fire blanket, eyewash station, fume hood, first aid kit. Common misconceptions: thinking safety equipment is only for emergencies. Practice should include safety equipment identification and scenarios appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Atoms and elements: Describe the structure of atoms, including the masses, electrical charges, and locations, of protons and neutrons in the nucleus and electrons in the electron cloud
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Explain how matter is classified as elements, compounds, or mixtures
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers atomic structure for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(A)). Students describe the components and arrangement of atoms. Key concepts include: protons (positive charge, in nucleus, determine element identity), neutrons (no charge, in nucleus, contribute to mass), electrons (negative charge, in electron cloud, determine chemical properties), atomic number = number of protons, and mass number = protons + neutrons. Vocabulary: atom, proton, neutron, electron, nucleus, electron cloud, atomic number, mass number, charge, element. Common misconceptions: thinking electrons orbit in fixed paths (they form clouds), confusing atomic number with mass number, and thinking atoms are mostly solid. Practice should include atomic model analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Atoms and elements: What are atoms and chemical elements?
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Explain how matter is classified as elements, compounds, or mixtures
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers atoms and chemical elements for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(A)). Students understand that atoms are the building blocks of matter and elements are pure substances made of one type of atom. Key concepts include: atoms are the smallest unit of an element, each element has a unique number of protons, elements are organized on the periodic table, atoms combine to form molecules and compounds, and the difference between elements and compounds. Vocabulary: atom, element, molecule, compound, proton, periodic table, chemical symbol, pure substance. Common misconceptions: confusing atoms with molecules, thinking elements and compounds are the same, and that atoms can be seen with regular microscopes. Practice should include classification exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Atoms and elements: Identify that protons determine an element's identity and valence electrons determine its chemical properties, including reactivity
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Use the periodic table to identify atoms in chemical reactions
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers how protons determine element identity and valence electrons determine chemical properties for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(B)). Key concepts include: the number of protons defines which element an atom is (atomic number), valence electrons (outermost electrons) determine how an atom bonds and reacts, atoms with full outer shells are stable (noble gases), and atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve stability. Vocabulary: proton, atomic number, valence electron, chemical property, reactivity, stability, noble gas, electron shell, bond. Common misconceptions: thinking neutrons determine element identity and confusing total electrons with valence electrons. Practice should include periodic table analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Periodic Table: Interpret the arrangement of the Periodic Table, including groups and periods, to explain how properties are used to classify elements
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Use the periodic table to identify atoms in chemical reactions
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers interpreting the Periodic Table for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(B)). Students understand how the table organizes elements by properties. Key concepts include: elements are arranged by atomic number, groups (columns) have similar chemical properties, periods (rows) represent electron shell levels, metals/nonmetals/metalloids regions, and how group number relates to valence electrons. Vocabulary: periodic table, group, period, atomic number, chemical property, metal, nonmetal, metalloid, valence electron, classify. Common misconceptions: thinking elements in the same period have similar properties and confusing groups with periods. Practice should include periodic table navigation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Chemical formulas: Recognize that chemical formulas are used to identify substances and determine the number of atoms of each element in chemical formulas containing subscripts
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Explain how matter is classified as elements, compounds, or mixtures
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers recognizing and interpreting chemical formulas for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(A)). Students read chemical formulas to identify substances and count atoms. Key concepts include: chemical symbols represent elements, subscripts indicate the number of atoms, no subscript means one atom, interpreting formulas like H2O (2 hydrogen, 1 oxygen), and distinguishing elements from compounds by their formulas. Vocabulary: chemical formula, element, compound, subscript, atom, molecule, chemical symbol. Common misconceptions: confusing subscripts with coefficients, thinking subscripts apply to all elements in the formula, and not understanding what formulas represent physically. Practice should include formula interpretation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Chemical formulas: How are substances represented by chemical formulas and models?
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Explain how matter is classified as elements, compounds, or mixtures
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers how substances are represented by chemical formulas and models for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(A)). Students connect chemical formulas to physical and ball-and-stick models. Key concepts include: chemical formulas show element types and quantities, ball-and-stick models show 3D arrangement, different representations serve different purposes, and connecting molecular models to written formulas. Vocabulary: chemical formula, model, ball-and-stick model, molecule, atom, bond, element, compound, representation. Common misconceptions: thinking models are exact replicas, confusing different model types, and not connecting formulas to structures. Practice should include model-formula matching appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Chemical formulas: Identify chemical formulas for ball-and-stick models
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Explain how matter is classified as elements, compounds, or mixtures
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers identifying chemical formulas from ball-and-stick models for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(A)). Students translate 3D molecular models into written chemical formulas. Key concepts include: each ball color represents a specific element, counting atoms of each element in the model, writing the chemical formula with proper subscripts, and recognizing common molecules from their models. Vocabulary: ball-and-stick model, chemical formula, element, atom, subscript, molecule, bond, identify. Common misconceptions: miscounting atoms, confusing element colors, and not using subscripts correctly. Practice should include model-to-formula conversion appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Chemical formulas: Describe the atomic composition of molecules
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Explain how matter is classified as elements, compounds, or mixtures
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers describing the atomic composition of molecules for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(A)). Students identify the types and numbers of atoms that make up molecules. Key concepts include: molecules are made of two or more atoms bonded together, reading chemical formulas to determine composition, distinguishing between molecular elements (O2) and compounds (H2O), and comparing atomic composition of different molecules. Vocabulary: molecule, atom, composition, element, compound, bond, chemical formula, subscript. Common misconceptions: thinking molecules are always compounds (some are molecular elements) and confusing atoms with molecules. Practice should include composition analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Chemical reactions: Investigate how evidence of chemical reactions indicates that new substances with different properties are formed and how that relates to the law of conservation of mass
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Investigate how mass is conserved in chemical reactions
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers evidence of chemical reactions and the law of conservation of mass for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(E)). Students identify evidence that new substances have formed and verify mass conservation. Key evidence includes: color change, gas production, temperature change, precipitate formation, and odor change. Key concepts include: matter is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions, total mass of reactants equals total mass of products, and new substances have different properties from the original. Vocabulary: chemical reaction, evidence, conservation of mass, reactant, product, precipitate, gas, temperature change. Common misconceptions: thinking mass can be created or destroyed in reactions and confusing physical with chemical changes. Practice should include reaction evidence analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Chemical reactions: Identify reactants and products
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Investigate how mass is conserved in chemical reactions
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers identifying reactants and products in chemical reactions for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(E)). Students distinguish between the substances that enter a reaction (reactants) and the substances that are produced (products). Key concepts include: reactants are on the left side of a chemical equation, products are on the right side, the arrow means yields, and new substances with new properties are formed. Vocabulary: reactant, product, chemical equation, yield, arrow, chemical reaction, substance, before, after. Common misconceptions: confusing reactants with products, thinking some matter disappears during reactions, and not recognizing that products have different properties. Practice should include equation reading appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Chemical reactions: Count atoms and molecules in chemical reactions
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Investigate how mass is conserved in chemical reactions
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers counting atoms and molecules in chemical reactions for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(E)). Students count the number of each type of atom on both sides of a chemical equation to check for balance. Key concepts include: reading subscripts and coefficients, coefficients multiply all atoms in a formula, subscripts indicate atoms within a molecule, and balanced equations have equal atoms on both sides. Vocabulary: atom, molecule, coefficient, subscript, balanced equation, reactant, product, chemical equation. Common misconceptions: confusing coefficients with subscripts, not multiplying coefficients through all atoms, and thinking unbalanced equations are valid. Practice should include atom counting exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Chemical reactions: Calculate amounts of reactants or products in chemical reactions
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Investigate how mass is conserved in chemical reactions
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers calculating amounts of reactants or products in chemical reactions for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(E)). Students use balanced equations and the law of conservation of mass to determine unknown amounts. Key concepts include: using ratios from balanced equations, applying conservation of mass (total reactant mass = total product mass), and solving for unknown quantities. Vocabulary: reactant, product, balanced equation, conservation of mass, ratio, calculate, chemical reaction, mass. Common misconceptions: not using balanced equations, ignoring conservation of mass, and errors in ratio calculations. Practice should include calculation problems appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Chemical reactions: Describe energy changes in chemical reactions
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Investigate how mass is conserved in chemical reactions
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers energy changes in chemical reactions for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(E)). Students understand that chemical reactions involve energy changes. Key concepts include: exothermic reactions release energy (feel hot), endothermic reactions absorb energy (feel cold), activation energy is needed to start reactions, and energy is stored in and released from chemical bonds. Vocabulary: exothermic, endothermic, activation energy, chemical bond, energy release, energy absorption, temperature change. Common misconceptions: thinking all reactions release energy, confusing temperature change direction with reaction type, and not understanding activation energy. Practice should include classifying reactions appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Chemical reactions: Compare physical and chemical changes
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Explain how matter is classified as elements, compounds, or mixtures
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers distinguishing between physical and chemical changes in matter for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.6(A)). Students identify and compare changes that alter appearance only (physical) versus changes that create new substances (chemical). Key concepts include: physical changes (melting, dissolving, cutting) do not create new substances, chemical changes (burning, rusting, cooking) produce new substances with different properties, evidence of chemical change (color change, gas production, temperature change, precipitate), and reversibility. Vocabulary: physical change, chemical change, substance, property, reversible, irreversible, precipitate, reaction. Common misconceptions: thinking all color changes are chemical and confusing dissolving (physical) with a chemical reaction. Practice should include classification activities appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Forces and motion: Demonstrate and calculate how unbalanced forces change the speed or direction of an object's motion
Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Calculate how acceleration depends on net force and mass using Newton's Second Law
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers how unbalanced forces change motion for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(A)). Students demonstrate and calculate force effects on objects. Key concepts include: Newton's Second Law (F = ma), unbalanced forces cause acceleration, balanced forces cause no change in motion, net force calculation, and connecting force to real-world applications. Vocabulary: unbalanced force, acceleration, net force, Newton's Second Law, mass, speed, direction, balanced force. Common misconceptions: thinking objects need force to keep moving and confusing mass with weight. Practice should include force calculation appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Forces and motion: How does mass affect force and acceleration?
Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Calculate how acceleration depends on net force and mass using Newton's Second Law
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between mass, force, and acceleration for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(A)). Students investigate how mass affects the force needed to accelerate an object. Key concepts include: Newton's Second Law (F = ma), greater mass requires greater force for the same acceleration, the same force produces less acceleration on more massive objects, and inverse proportionality between mass and acceleration at constant force. Vocabulary: mass, force, acceleration, Newton's Second Law, proportional, inverse, relationship, kilogram, Newton. Common misconceptions: confusing mass with weight, thinking heavier objects always move slower, and not understanding the proportional relationships. Practice should include quantitative analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Forces and motion: Balanced and unbalanced forces
Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Calculate how acceleration depends on net force and mass using Newton's Second Law
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers balanced and unbalanced forces for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(A)). Students distinguish between balanced forces (net force = 0) and unbalanced forces (net force not 0). Key concepts include: balanced forces result in no change in motion (object at rest stays at rest, moving object continues at same speed), unbalanced forces cause acceleration (speeding up, slowing down, or changing direction), calculating net force, and real-world examples. Vocabulary: balanced force, unbalanced force, net force, acceleration, equilibrium, friction, gravity, Newton. Common misconceptions: thinking balanced forces mean no forces are acting and that objects need constant force to move. Practice should include force analysis appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Speed, velocity, and acceleration: Differentiate between speed, velocity, and acceleration
Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Calculate how acceleration depends on net force and mass
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers speed, velocity, and acceleration for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(A)). Students distinguish between these three related but distinct concepts. Key concepts include: speed = distance/time (scalar), velocity = displacement/time with direction (vector), acceleration = change in velocity/time, positive acceleration means speeding up, negative acceleration (deceleration) means slowing down. Vocabulary: speed, velocity, acceleration, scalar, vector, direction, distance, displacement, time, rate. Common misconceptions: using speed and velocity interchangeably, thinking acceleration always means speeding up, and not including direction for velocity. Practice should include differentiation exercises appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Speed, velocity, and acceleration: Calculate velocity from time and distance
Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Calculate how acceleration depends on net force and mass
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers calculating velocity from time and distance for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(A)). Students use the formula v = d/t to calculate velocity. Key concepts include: velocity includes both speed and direction, using consistent units, solving word problems, and interpreting results. Vocabulary: velocity, distance, time, speed, formula, units, meters per second, direction. Common misconceptions: confusing speed with velocity, using inconsistent units, and not including direction. Practice should include calculation problems appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Speed, velocity, and acceleration: Calculate distance from velocity and time
Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Calculate how acceleration depends on net force and mass
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers calculating distance from velocity and time for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(A)). Students rearrange the velocity formula to d = vt to find distance. Key concepts include: using the formula d = v x t, ensuring consistent units, solving word problems, and interpreting results in context. Vocabulary: distance, velocity, time, formula, units, meters, seconds, calculate. Common misconceptions: using wrong units, confusing distance with displacement, and rearranging the formula incorrectly. Practice should include distance calculation problems appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Speed, velocity, and acceleration: Calculate time from velocity and distance
Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Calculate how acceleration depends on net force and mass
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers calculating time from velocity and distance for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(A)). Students rearrange the velocity formula to t = d/v to find time. Key concepts include: using the formula t = d/v, ensuring consistent units, solving word problems, and interpreting results. Vocabulary: time, distance, velocity, formula, units, seconds, minutes, hours. Common misconceptions: dividing in wrong order and not converting units. Practice should include time calculation problems appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Calculate how acceleration depends on net force and mass
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers calculating velocity, distance, and time using the v = d/t relationship for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(A)). Vocabulary: velocity, distance, time, formula, units, rearrange, solve, speed. Common misconceptions: choosing the wrong formula, unit inconsistencies, and confusing which variable to solve for. Practice should include mixed calculation problems appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Speed, velocity, and acceleration: Calculate velocity, distance, and time II
Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Calculate how acceleration depends on net force and mass
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers calculating velocity, distance, and time using the v = d/t relationship for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(A)). Vocabulary: velocity, distance, time, formula, units, rearrange, solve, speed. Common misconceptions: choosing the wrong formula, unit inconsistencies, and confusing which variable to solve for. Practice should include mixed calculation problems appropriate for 13-14 year olds.

Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Speed, velocity, and acceleration: Identify whether objects are accelerating
Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Calculate how acceleration depends on net force and mass
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers identifying whether objects are accelerating for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(A)). Students determine if objects are accelerating based on changes in speed or direction. Key concepts include: acceleration is any change in velocity (speed or direction), constant speed in a straight line = no acceleration, speeding up or slowing down = acceleration, changing direction at constant speed = acceleration. Vocabulary: acceleration, velocity, speed, direction, constant, change, deceleration. Common misconceptions: thinking only speeding up is acceleration, not recognizing direction change as acceleration, and confusing constant speed with zero acceleration. Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Newton's laws: Investigate and describe applications of Newton's three laws of motion such as in vehicle restraints, sports activities, amusement park rides, Earth's tectonic activities, and rocket launches
Science - Force, motion, and energy.teks_description: Investigate how Newton's three laws act simultaneously in systems
Science - Force, motion, and energy.prompt: This topic covers applications of Newton's three laws of motion for Grade 8 science students (TEKS 8.7(B)). Students apply all three laws to real-world situations. Key concepts include: First Law (inertia - objects resist changes in motion), Second Law (F = ma - force equals mass times acceleration), Third Law (action-reaction - every force has an equal and opposite reaction), and applications in vehicle restraints, sports, amusement rides, tectonics, and rockets. Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Newton's laws: Predict forces using Newton's third law

Science - Force, motion, and energy.skill: Newton's laws: Collisions and car safety features
